notes: Assertion 1 (doc fetch attempted) is perfectly discriminating — passes 100% with skill, fails 100% without. This is the key behavioral change the skill produces.
Assertions 2-3 are non-discriminating — pass in both configurations. They serve as quality floors (Claude produces valid KQL either way) rather than differentiators.

The real quality gap not captured by assertions: without-skill runs used wrong NodeProperties paths (.data.* vs .rawData.*) and wrong AIAgentsInfo column names (Tools vs AgentToolsDetails). These would return 0 rows in a real tenant.
With-skill CloudAppEvents query incorporated three Defender-native anomaly signals (UncommonForUser, LastSeenForUser, IsAnonymousProxy) learned from the doc fetch — not captured by assertions.
With-skill runs are ~5x slower and ~2.7x more token-intensive — cost of thorough schema lookup, doc fetch, and live sampling before writing the query.

Eval 5 with-skill (355s, 40 tool calls) was significantly more exploratory than eval 4 (189s, 15 calls) and eval 6 (153s, 11 calls) — AIAgentsInfo required more iteration to discover actual action.$kind values and syntax limitations.
